meta.note: All cards are drawn from the lecture. Import into Anki/Quizlet as Q/A pairs.

cards.q: What does epidemiology study?
cards.a: The distribution and prevalence of disease in a population — how much, where, and in whom.

cards.q: Does epidemiology focus on the individual or the population?
cards.a: The population as a whole. The individual is cared for by the individual, then the physician/care team.

cards.q: What is the ultimate goal of epidemiology?
cards.a: Intervention — find the pattern a disease follows, then step in to stop the spread.

cards.q: Why does the deck stress the role of nurses?
cards.a: Nurses are on the front line — they see the sickest patients first (ER) and often spot an epidemic beginning.

cards.q: Who is the 'father of epidemiology,' and what did he do?
cards.a: John Snow — he mapped London cholera cases to a water pump and removed the Broad Street pump handle (the intervention).

cards.q: What is patient zero (the initial case)?
cards.a: The first case in an outbreak, found by clustering cases and tracing backward.

cards.q: What is etiology?
cards.a: The study of the cause of a disease. Find the cause, then intervene.

cards.q: What three things do epidemiologists always ask?
cards.a: Place, People, and Time — where you were, who you were with, and when/how long.

cards.q: Why is most epidemiologic data often 'a year behind'?
cards.a: Data is collected passively (reported by facilities); real-time tracking is too expensive, so trends are studied from last year's data.

cards.q: Where do reportable diseases get sent, and what does the CDC publish weekly?
cards.a: To the CDC (Atlanta), which publishes the Morbidity and Mortality Report and shares it with the WHO.

cards.q: Infection vs. disease vs. infectious disease?
cards.a: Infection = an organism grows in the body and causes symptoms. Disease = altered function NOT from a microbe. Infectious disease = microbe-caused, typically chronic.

cards.q: Give an example of a 'disease' that is not an infection.
cards.a: Myasthenia gravis or anorexia nervosa — an alteration of tissue/metabolic function, not caused by a microbe.

cards.q: Disease is also referred to as ____?
cards.a: Morbidity.

cards.q: Why is strep throat 'infectious disease' but not the main focus of epidemiology?
cards.a: It's short-lived (a few days on antibiotics). Epidemiology cares most about chronic ones like TB and AIDS.

cards.q: Name the 5 ways diseases are classified.
cards.a: By causative agent, body system affected, longevity/severity, how they're spread, and their effect on populations.

cards.q: Chronic vs. acute?
cards.a: Chronic = long-lasting; acute = very severe and very fast.

cards.q: Define sporadic.
cards.a: A few scattered, unrelated cases — no real effect on the population.

cards.q: Define epidemic.
cards.a: A short-term increase in cases of a disease in a given population.

cards.q: Define endemic.
cards.a: An agent present in a given population or location all the time (e.g., Coccidioides in the SW US).

cards.q: Define pandemic.
cards.a: A worldwide increase — cases on 6 continents (Antarctica doesn't count). Examples: HIV/AIDS, TB, COVID-19.

cards.q: Give the professor's endemic examples.
cards.a: Ebola in West Africa, Lyme disease in Texas, rabies in Austin (bats), Coccidioides in the SW US, diabetes in Hispanic & Black populations.

cards.q: Which current pandemic did he want added to HIV/AIDS and TB?
cards.a: COVID-19.

cards.q: What is the reproductive number (R0)?
cards.a: The expected number of new cases generated by one case in a fully susceptible population — a measure of contagiousness.

cards.q: Flu vs. COVID-19 — which has the higher R0?
cards.a: COVID-19 — a much higher R0, making it more contagious and more severe than influenza.

cards.q: Morbidity vs. mortality?
cards.a: Morbidity = number of cases (people sick). Mortality = number of deaths. Both are usually given as rates.

cards.q: If morbidity is 10 per 100,000 in a city of 1,000,000, how many cases is that?
cards.a: 100 cases (10 segments of 100,000 × 10).

cards.q: Prevalence vs. incidence?
cards.a: Prevalence = total number of cases. Incidence = number of NEW cases (tells you how fast it's spreading).

cards.q: Prevalence 50, incidence 2 — should you worry?
cards.a: No — only 2 of 50 are new, so the outbreak is waning.

cards.q: Prevalence 50, incidence 48 — should you worry?
cards.a: Yes — 48 of 50 are brand-new cases; you have a big problem right now.

cards.q: What is confounding data?
cards.a: A distortion that makes data misleading — e.g., over-testing during the Gardasil debate made HPV look like the #1 STI.

cards.q: Which STI is actually the most common for the average city?
cards.a: Chlamydia (not HPV — that was confounded by over-testing).

cards.q: What is the 'first rule of epidemiology' about communicating numbers?
cards.a: Don't put the public in an agitated state — speak in rates ('10 per 100,000') so professionals read the signal without panic.

cards.q: Name the 5 stages of infectious disease.
cards.a: Incubation, prodromal, illness, decline, convalescence.

cards.q: What is the incubation period, and why does it matter?
cards.a: The time from exposure until symptoms appear. You can be contagious before you feel sick, so it's key to intervening.

cards.q: What are the three possible outcomes of an infectious disease?
cards.a: Recover (convalesce), death, or impairment (cognitive or physical, sometimes lifelong).

cards.q: Can the effects of an infectious disease last after the agent is gone?
cards.a: Yes — e.g., meningococcal meningitis or encephalitis can leave lasting cognitive/physical impairment.

cards.q: Name the three groups of transmission.
cards.a: Contact (direct/indirect/droplet), vehicle (air/water/food), and vector (biological/mechanical).

cards.q: Direct vs. indirect contact — examples?
cards.a: Direct = sex; indirect = shaking hands or touching a contaminated object. Droplet = a sneeze.

cards.q: What is a fomite?
cards.a: An inanimate object that can transmit an infectious agent — a doorknob, a phone, a contaminated object.

cards.q: What are portals of entry and exit?
cards.a: How a pathogen gets into and out of the body — often the same route, usually via secretions/excretions.

cards.q: Communicable vs. non-communicable disease?
cards.a: Communicable = transmissible host-to-host (incl. mother→fetus). Non-communicable = acquired but not passed on (e.g., Lyme disease).

cards.q: What does 'contagious' mean?
cards.a: Highly communicable.

cards.q: How do wet markets contribute to novel viruses?
cards.a: Close animal–human proximity allows an exchange of proteins → mutations → novel viruses (novel coronavirus, novel avian flu).

cards.q: How can an infectious agent cross the globe?
cards.a: Overlapping bird-migration flyways — or faster, a sick person on an airplane (a doctor carried Ebola from Liberia to New Jersey).

cards.q: Why do infectious diseases come in 'waves,' and what stops them?
cards.a: A mildly-ill person unknowingly exposes many others, who each seed the next wave. Social distancing, self-quarantine, and masks cut those exposures and break the wave.

cards.q: Reservoir vs. source?
cards.a: Reservoir = the natural habitat a pathogen originates from (living or nonliving). Source = the specific individual/object you actually got it from.

cards.q: What is a carrier?
cards.a: A person who harbors an agent and can spread it, often with little or no symptoms.

cards.q: What group is the #1 carrier?
cards.a: Children ('kiddos') — they carry strep throat, hand-foot-and-mouth, and more.

cards.q: Biological vs. mechanical vector?
cards.a: Biological = actively part of the pathogen's life cycle; takes a blood meal (mosquito → malaria). Mechanical = just carries it, not infected (housefly).

cards.q: What is a zoonosis?
cards.a: An infection indigenous to animals but naturally transmissible to humans (at least 150 worldwide; humans don't sustain the microbe).

cards.q: Zoonosis vs. zoonoses — is the 'e' a typo?
cards.a: No — zoonosis is singular, zoonoses is plural.

cards.q: Name two nonliving reservoirs.
cards.a: Soil and water (e.g., contaminated Katrina floodwater).

cards.q: Which age groups are most susceptible to infection, and why?
cards.a: The very old (lose the ability to fight infection) and the very young (adaptive immunity not developed → rely on cell-mediated immunity).

cards.q: List host-susceptibility factors.
cards.a: Overall health, age extremes, immunization status, genetic background, culture, and religion.

cards.q: What time of year is worst for infectious disease, and why?
cards.a: Summer — people are out socializing. (July 4th is the #1 food-poisoning holiday, then Thanksgiving.)

cards.q: Why might someone raised on a farm have a stronger immune system than a lifelong city dweller?
cards.a: Early exposure to many microbes trains immunity (the hygiene idea) — which is also why letting kids get a little dirty helps build defenses.

cards.q: Name pathogen traits that influence an epidemic.
cards.a: Adherence, invasiveness, mutation rate (antigenic variation), intracellular survival, toxin production, infectious dose, incubation period.

cards.q: Compare the infectious dose of Salmonella vs. Shigella.
cards.a: Salmonella needs ~100,000 cells; Shigella needs only ~10 — making Shigella far more virulent.

cards.q: What is antigenic variation, and why does it matter?
cards.a: Mutation that changes a pathogen's surface — you treat one form and it changes (COVID had 200+ strains; UK/South African/Brazil variants were dangerous).

cards.q: Name intracellular pathogens that evade immunity.
cards.a: HIV/AIDS, malaria, chlamydia, rickettsia.

cards.q: List conditions that weaken host defenses.
cards.a: Age extremes, genetic/acquired immune defects (AIDS), surgery/transplants, cancer/liver disease/diabetes, chemo/immunosuppressants, and secondary infections.

cards.q: Why are prophylactic antibiotics given after surgery?
cards.a: Surgery creates an artificial portal of entry, so antibiotics are given to prevent infection.

cards.q: Name pathogen survival strategies.
cards.a: Cyst formation, endospores, mutation, intracellular life, complex life cycles, and capsules (bacteria/yeast).

cards.q: Name the main ways to control an epidemic.
cards.a: Reduce the source (quarantine, destroy infected hosts), disrupt transmission (destroy vectors, pasteurization, water treatment), immunize, hand-wash, and do surveillance.

cards.q: What is the best way to protect an entire population?
cards.a: Immunization — have as many citizens immunized as possible.

cards.q: Give the pasteurization temperatures.
cards.a: Standard: 60 °C for 30 minutes. Flash: 72 °C for 15 seconds.

cards.q: Which two diseases can you be arrested for actively spreading?
cards.a: Tuberculosis and AIDS. There is no such law for COVID-19.

cards.q: What is a nosocomial infection, and how common is it?
cards.a: An infection acquired during a hospital stay — about 2–4 million cases a year in the US.

cards.q: Name the 3 types of nosocomial infection.
cards.a: Exogenous (from the healthcare environment), endogenous (from your own microbiota), iatrogenic (from a medical procedure).

cards.q: What is the single most effective way to reduce nosocomial infections?
cards.a: Hand washing.

cards.q: What are Universal Precautions (UPs)?
cards.a: CDC guidelines to assume every patient specimen could be infectious and use body-substance isolation (BSI) techniques.

cards.q: Symptom vs. sign vs. syndrome?
cards.a: Symptom = subjective (patient-reported). Sign = objective (measured by a practitioner). Syndrome = the cluster of signs + symptoms that defines a disease.

cards.q: What does asymptomatic (subclinical) mean?
cards.a: No symptoms, but signs of infection may still be present.

cards.q: Give the reportable-disease timelines.
cards.a: Immediately: anthrax. Same day: TB. End of week: COVID-19. End of month: STIs.
